Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Two white panels separated by a purple vertical divider bar - Left panel: green 3×3 hollow square (center W=white at [31,19]), charcoal 5×5 hollow square - Right panel: green 3×3 hollow square (center B=black at [31,28]), charcoal 5×5 hollow square - Bottom charcoal bar (HUD element) - Actions: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT/MOUSE available - Goal unknown - likely involves matching or swapping the green square contents

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after clicking on [31, 19]. Let me compare what changed. I see a new node id=14 (W, 1 pixel at [63, 63]) which is on the bottom charcoal bar - this might be a HUD change. But the main objects seem unchanged. Let me check more carefully what changed by comparing the before and after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after the click
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

# Check segmentation differences
before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}

print("Before nodes:", sorted(before_nodes.keys()))
print("After nodes:", sorted(after_nodes.keys()))
print("New nodes:", set(after_nodes.keys()) - set(before_nodes.keys()))
print("Removed nodes:", set(before_nodes.keys()) - set(after_nodes.keys()))

# Check for boundary/color changes in existing nodes
for nid in before_nodes:
    if nid in after_nodes:
        bn = before_nodes[nid]
        an = after_nodes[nid]
        if bn['boundary'] != an['boundary'] or bn['color'] != an['color'] or bn['pixels'] != an['pixels']:
            print(f"Node {nid} changed: {bn['color']}->{an['color']}, pixels {bn['pixels']}->{an['pixels']}")

# Check the bottom bar area in ascii
lines_before = before.ascii.split('\n')
lines_after = after.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nBottom row before:", repr(lines_before[63]))
print("Bottom row after:", repr(lines_after[63]))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So clicking on [31, 19] (the white center of the left green square) only changed the bottom bar - one charcoal pixel at position [63, 63] turned white. The bottom bar is a HUD/progress bar. This means clicking on the white center of the left green square was a valid action that advanced the progress bar by 1.
Let me try clicking on other objects to understand what each does. The goal seems to be to fill the bottom progress bar by clicking the right objects. Let me try the black center at [31, 28].

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the black center of the right green square at [31, 28]
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 28}])
print("Result:", last_action_result)

# Check what changed
lines_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Bottom row:", repr(lines_after[63]))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Node count:", len(seg['nodes']))
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
